title: GDPR DPIA Completion Tracking Against High-Risk Processing
exclusions: Processing activities assessed below the DPIA threshold via documented screening criteria; activities covered by group-level DPIAs with explicit scope statements.
evidence: DPIA tracking dashboard export showing high-risk activities count, DPIA coverage percentage, average completion time, and activities awaiting assessment with priority ranking.

compliance.assurance_rationale: GDPR Art.35(7) — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.35(7) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.32: GDPR DPIA Completion Tracking Against High-Risk Processing.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.1.32' running on gdpr_ropa_register.csv, gdpr_dpia_register.csv, change records snow:change_request, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Create a test processing activity with risk score high and no associated DPIA record; verify the DPIA tracking search identifies the uncovered high-risk activity and generates a governance alert.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Create a test processing activity with risk score high and a linked, completed DPIA with all Art.35(7) elements documented; verify no DPIA coverage gap is reported.

spl:
| inputlookup gdpr_ropa_register.csv WHERE high_risk="true"
| lookup gdpr_dpia_register.csv processing_activity OUTPUT dpia_status, dpia_completed_date
| eval dpi_gap=case(
    dpia_status="completed", "OK",
    dpia_status="in_progress", "IN_PROGRESS",
    1=1, "MISSING")
| join processing_activity type=left [
    search index=itsm sourcetype="snow:change_request" earliest=-90d
    | where match(lower(short_description),"(?i)go.live|launch|migrate")
    | stats latest(start_date) as last_major_change by cmdb_ci
    | rename cmdb_ci as system_name
]
| where dpi_gap!="OK"
| table processing_activity, system_name, dpi_gap, dpia_status, last_major_change
description: Where processing is likely to result in a high risk, the controller shall carry out a DPIA prior to processing. This use case reconciles the ROPA, system go-live dates, and DPIA register to flag processing that started or changed materially without a completed DPIA.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for GDPR compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes itsm and sourcetypes snow:change_request. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Keep `high_risk` flags on ROPA entries per Art. 35(3); (2) join DPIA register by processing activity; (3) correlate major changes with DPIA refresh policy; (4) alert DPO on `MISSING` before production changes; (5) align with UC-22.1.14 for consolidated DPIA coverage views. Indexes required: `index=itsm`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=snow:change_request`; Lookups: `gdpr_dpia_register`, `gdpr_dpia_register.csv`, `gdpr_ropa_register`, `gdpr_ropa_register.csv`.
visualization: Table (missing DPIAs), Single value (high-risk gaps), Pie chart (status distribution), Timeline (go-live vs DPIA dates). Key fields: `dpi_gap`, `dpia_status`, `last_major_change`, `processing_activity`, `system_name`.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Change.All_Changes by All_Changes.user, All_Changes.object, All_Changes.command | sort - count
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Risk score recalculation pending** — Processing activities awaiting annual risk reassessment may show outdated scores; check the assessment schedule for pending reviews.

2. **DPIA threshold interpretation changes** — Updated supervisory authority guidance on high-risk thresholds may reclassify previously low-risk activities; allow a reasonable adoption period.

3. **Pre-GDPR processing grandfathering** — Activities operational before 25 May 2018 that have not materially changed may have lighter DPIA documentation per transitional provisions.

4. **Grouped DPIA coverage** — Multiple processing activities covered under a single DPIA may individually appear as lacking coverage; check the DPIA scope statement for grouped activities.

5. **Processing volume changes** — Activities that crossed the high-risk threshold due to organic growth may have a documented timeline for DPIA completion following the threshold trigger.

**Operational suppression:** Maintain a lookup table (`gdpr_approved_exceptions.csv`) mapping known legitimate activities by process owner, approval reference, and expiry date. Filter the SPL results against this lookup to suppress documented exceptions and reduce alert fatigue. Review and rotate entries quarterly.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase app ID 1928) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `itsm` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `gdpr_dpia_register.csv`, `gdpr_ropa_register.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('gdpr_dpia_register.csv','gdpr_ropa_register.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.35(7)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that GDPR Art.35(7) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.32: GDPR DPIA Completion Tracking Against High-Risk Processing." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase app ID 1928), landing events in `itsm` under `snow:change_request`. REST-polled ticket tables produce 500-5000 records per sync per table; at 300s interval expect ~3-8 GB/month for a mid-size service desk.

Configure the input through Settings > Data Inputs > ServiceNow Service Account. A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[snow://{instance_slug}]
url = https://<instance>.service-now.com
table = {table_name}
interval = 300
index = itsm
sourcetype = {sourcetype}
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `number, state, priority, assignment_group, opened_at, closed_at, sys_updated_on, caller_id, category, subcategory`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=itsm earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Keep `high_risk` flags on ROPA entries per Art. 35. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** join DPIA register by processing activity. The register referenced here is primary evidence — treat changes to it with the same discipline as code changes.

• **Hint 3:** correlate major changes with DPIA refresh policy. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** alert DPO on `MISSING` before production changes. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** align with UC-22.1.14 for consolidated DPIA coverage views. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "GDPR DPIA Completion Tracking Against High-Risk Processing," the aggregation produces the risk posture — the per-row values should reconcile to the organisation's risk register entries, not stand alone as engineering metrics.

This search begins with `| inputlookup gdpr_ropa_register.csv` — the base dataset is a CSV/KV Store lookup, not live event data. This inverts the usual Splunk assumption: the detection does not stream from an index, it iterates a register. The register itself is the authoritative source of truth (contracts, policies, sign-offs, attestations) and the search joins it against indexed events to find gaps.

Lookup authority: treat `gdpr_ropa_register.csv` as a primary compliance artifact. Its contents ARE the answer the regulator will ask for ('show me your vendor list,' 'show me your CDE inventory,' 'show me your policy exceptions'). Implement the following controls on the lookup itself, not just the search:

• Version the CSV in a git repository with a mandatory pull-request review for every change.
• Maintain a signed-record trail: who changed what, when, and with what approval. A lookup with a silent edit history is an audit liability.
• Automate a nightly diff: `| inputlookup gdpr_ropa_register.csv | eval hash=md5(...) | outputlookup append=true gdpr_ropa_register_audit.csv` — this produces an immutable record of the register's state over time.
• Lock the lookup with role-based access: only the content owner has `list_inputs` + `write_file` on the path; everyone else is read-only.

The live-data side of the join (across the last 90 days) is the detection trigger — when the register and the telemetry disagree, that's a finding.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Change` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Change.All_Changes by All_Changes.user, All_Changes.object, All_Changes.command | sort - count
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Change` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.1.32: GDPR DPIA Completion Tracking Against High-Risk Processing]
cron_schedule = 0 3 1 * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -90d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.1.32,reg=GDPR
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=itsm sourcetype=snow:change_request earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `dpi_gap`, `dpia_status`, `last_major_change`, `processing_activity`, `system_name`. Run:

```spl
index=itsm sourcetype=snow:change_request earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table dpi_gap, dpia_status, last_major_change, processing_activity, system_name
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `gdpr_dpia_register.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup gdpr_dpia_register.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.1.32: GDPR evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (`missing DPIAs`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Single-value KPI (`high-risk gaps`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'GDPR current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.

**Panel 3 — Pie chart (`status distribution`):** Shows proportion across categorical dimension. Use sparingly — pie charts are poor at small-slice comparison; a bar chart is often clearer.

**Panel 4 — Time trend (`go-live vs DPIA dates`):** Place in the main content row. Use `timechart span=1d` aggregated appropriately for the regulation's review cadence (daily for PCI/HIPAA, weekly for ISO-style controls). Annotate the chart with control-change dates so auditors can see when mitigations took effect.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**GDPR-specific failure modes:**

• Lawful basis drift: a detection that worked when the processing activity had consent as its lawful basis becomes misleading when the business team silently changes the RoPA (Record of Processing Activities) entry to 'legitimate interest.' Every GDPR compliance search should reference the RoPA entry ID in its comments and re-review the detection when the RoPA is revised — at minimum annually.

• Data Subject Rights timers: Article 12(3) requires response to access/erasure/portability requests within one month. Detections that measure DSR fulfilment must use business-day clocks (not wall-clock 30 days) for regulators that enforce the distinction; the threshold value in the search should align with the local Supervisory Authority's interpretation, not the ISO 8601 literal.

• Cross-border transfer fragility: Schrems II invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield in 2020 and the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (2023) replaced it; any detection involving transfer monitoring must key off the current adequacy decision list, not a hardcoded country allowlist. Maintain `gdpr_adequacy_list.csv` and refresh when the EDPB updates the adequacy register.

• Breach notification 72-hour window (Art. 33): the clock starts at awareness, not at the forensically-confirmed moment of compromise. Detections must capture the first-awareness event (SOC alert acknowledgment, first ticket open, first email notification) — not just the incident-resolved timestamp — or the notification SLA metric will understate breaches that took 72h+1h to confirm.
grandmaExplanation: We where processing is likely to result in a high risk, the controller shall carry out a DPIA prior to processing so we can show we meet our privacy duties when people ask for proof.
